channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: The Invoice said Toys
item.description: Brazil’s vape ban has not stopped demand — it has pushed the market into paperwork tricks, border routes and online delivery networks. Products move under labels such as toys, electronics or accessories, leaving consumers with no reliable way to verify origin, contents or safety. Prohibition removes oversight while preserving access, creating a market where trust replaces regulation. Harm reduction policy should bring products into accountable systems, not leave users dependent on opacity. (Published on June 15, 2026bydisobedientmargins.com)

item.title: US Regulation of Nicotine Pouches & E-Cigarettes: With Special Guest Laura Leigh Oyler
item.description: US regulation of nicotine pouches and e-cigarettes remains one of the most contested areas of tobacco harm reduction policy. Complex approval pathways and uneven enforcement can make it harder for lower-risk products to reach adult smokers through legal, regulated channels. At the same time, consumer demand continues to shift towards smoke-free alternatives. A more effective framework would protect young people while ensuring that adult smokers have realistic access to products that reduce exposure to combustion. (Published on June 15, 2026byyoutube.com)

item.title: 'Safer nicotine products will replace combustible cigarettes’ - Michael Russell Oration 2026 #GFN26
item.description: The future of tobacco control depends on whether public health accepts the shift from smoking to safer nicotine products. Combustible cigarettes cause the greatest harm, while non-combustible alternatives can reduce exposure and offer more realistic routes away from smoking. The Michael Russell Oration at GFN26 reinforces a central harm reduction principle: people do not need to stop using nicotine to dramatically reduce risk. Regulation should accelerate the end of smoking, not protect the cigarette market from disruption. (Published on June 15, 2026bygfn.tv)
